headline: GRAINS DRIFT AS CRUDE CRASHES THREE PERCENT
subheadline: Livestock complex extends breakdown while energy costs collapse on Hormuz reopening hopes.
lead: Corn closed $4.745, down a half cent, marking the fourth straight session inside a six-cent band. The real story isn't grain drift, it's crude crashing 2.6% to $97.39 as three tankers successfully exited the Strait of Hormuz with their transponders dark. Energy's retreat is pulling input costs lower, but the livestock complex can't find a floor.
the_takeaway: Wait. Quiet days price the next move, not this one.
teaser: Range-bound grains watch crude crash while cattle complex searches for support.

one_number.unit: supertankers that exited Hormuz with transponders off
one_number.context: Iraqi and Emirati crude made it through the strait last week, the first confirmed exits since the crisis began. Oil markets are reading this as proof the chokepoint isn't sealed. WTI's 2.6% drop says the premium is starting to leak out of energy.

sections.title: Grains Mark Time
sections.body: Corn held $4.75, soybeans gained a nickel to $12.22, wheat flat at $6.24. The action is what didn't happen: no fund flow, no weather premium, no export surprise. **Range-bound isn't drama, but it's information**. The funds aren't selling, the commercials aren't buying, and planting pace data Thursday will either break the stalemate or extend it. December corn at $4.97 shows 22 cents of carry, exactly where prevent-plant calculations get real.
sections.catalyst: Upcoming China trade talks mentioned in soybean short covering
sections.bottom_line: Consolidation ahead of Thursday's planting data, carry structure intact.

sections.title: Livestock Extends Breakdown
sections.body: Live cattle dropped another half percent to $248.90, feeders matched the decline at $364.23. Both contracts are testing new monthly lows with **no technical support until $245 in lives, $355 in feeders**. The breakdown that started Thursday is methodical, not panicked. Hogs fell eight-tenths percent to $98.62, extending Friday's crash. Box beef cutouts haven't budged, suggesting this is fund liquidation, not demand destruction.
sections.bottom_line: Systematic breakdown continues, next support levels 3-4% lower.

sections.title: Energy Costs Collapse
sections.body: WTI crude crashed 2.6% to $97.39 after three supertankers successfully exited the Strait of Hormuz with transponders off, carrying Iraqi and Emirati oil. **The market is reading this as proof the chokepoint isn't sealed**. Natural gas dropped seven-tenths percent to $2.79. The energy premium that's been inflating input costs since March is starting to leak out. Diesel prices should follow crude lower this week.
sections.catalyst: Three tankers successfully exited Strait of Hormuz with transponders off
sections.bottom_line: Hormuz exits suggest chokepoint remains navigable, premium deflating.

outside_the_pit.title: USDA Researchers Refuse Kansas City Relocation
outside_the_pit.body: Seventy-six percent of USDA researchers told their union they won't relocate to Kansas City under the agency's latest reorganization push. The 2019 move gutted ERS and NIFA when 85% of staff quit rather than relocate. Another brain drain could delay critical crop reports during planting season.

outside_the_pit.title: China's Car Sales Crater on Fuel Costs
outside_the_pit.body: Chinese auto sales dropped 21.5% in April as higher gasoline prices crushed demand for internal combustion vehicles. EV sales couldn't offset the decline. China's the world's largest soybean buyer, and when their consumers feel energy price pressure, it shows up in our export pace eventually.

outside_the_pit.title: Iowa Pseudorabies Detection Halts Export Certification
outside_the_pit.body: APHIS suspended certification that Iowa is free of pseudorabies after the first commercial detection in 20 years. Export markets for Iowa pork are now complicated until quarantined premises are cleared. The detection was contained, but the paperwork disruption hits during peak marketing season.

spread_to_watch.label: WTI crude / Natural gas ratio
spread_to_watch.commentary: When crude crashes harder than natural gas, it usually means the geopolitical premium is coming out faster than the seasonal demand. The Hormuz exits are deflating oil's war premium while nat gas holds closer to fundamentals.

basis.headline: **Eastern Belt corn basis steady** as ethanol margins improve
basis.body: Lower crude prices are improving ethanol crack spreads, keeping Eastern Belt corn basis firm even as futures drift. **Western Belt staying soft**, consistent with the seasonal. Soybean basis quiet ahead of Thursday's crush margin data. Oil basis tracking the futures, no independent strength.

weekly_thread.question: Will planting data Thursday show the Belt is still on pace or falling behind?
weekly_thread.status_text: The market's been pricing no weather premium since corn hit 22 cents of carry. Thursday's USDA Crop Progress will either confirm the Belt is on schedule or start adding prevent-plant premium to the calendar spread.

the_more_you_know.title: When tankers go dark, markets listen
the_more_you_know.body: Three supertankers successfully exiting the Strait of Hormuz with their transponders off might sound like cloak-and-dagger stuff, but it's how global energy markets test geopolitical bottlenecks. When ships can navigate a 'closed' waterway by going dark, it proves the closure isn't absolute. Oil traders price this information instantly. WTI's 2.6% crash today reflects that realization. The energy premium in your input costs is starting to deflate.

watch_list.desc: China trade talks begin, watch soybean overnight response
Box beef cutout, lives need demand confirmation above $248
USDA Crop Progress: corn below 45% planted adds weather premium
Weekly export sales: beans under 400K MT keeps chart in charge

daily_quote.text: If we throw nature out the window, she comes back in the door with a pitchfork.

source_summary: CME futures data, USDA reports, energy market analysis, trade flow monitoring
